_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2
title: Simple interest, the warmup formula
deck: Multiply principal by rate by time. That is the whole formula. Simple interest grows in a straight line, with no compounding and no surprises.
objectives: Apply the simple interest formula I = Prt and the accumulated-amount formula A = P(1 + rt) to short-term lending and saving problems.

content.text: Imagine buying a 26-week Treasury bill at the next government auction. We pay $9,775 today, and twenty-six weeks from now the Treasury sends us $10,000. No coupon payments arrive in the middle, no interest is added to the account on any monthly schedule. The single $225 difference between purchase price and payout is the entire interest the investment will ever earn.

That kind of transaction, where the interest is computed once on the original principal and never on previously earned interest, is called simple interest. It is the warmup formula for the whole chapter: short to write, easy to apply, and accurate for short-term arrangements like Treasury bills, brief promissory notes, and most introductory savings problems. Once it is in our hands, the more complicated machinery of Section 3.3 will feel like a small modification rather than a new idea.
3.2.1  Simple interest grows in a straight line

In a simple-interest arrangement, the interest paid in each time period is calculated only on the original principal. The same dollar amount of interest accrues every year, every month, every day, regardless of how much interest has already accumulated. Because each period adds the same constant, the balance climbs in a perfectly straight line on a graph of dollars versus time.

content.caption: Figure 3.2. Five years of $1,000 at 5% simple interest. Each year adds the same $50 of interest, so the top of the bars climbs in a perfect straight line.

content.text:
Definition 3.2. Simple interest is interest calculated only on the original principal, never on previously earned interest. With principal P, decimal rate r, and time t measured in the same units as the rate,
I = P r t    and    A = P + I = P(1 + r t)

Margin noteThe compact form A = P(1 + rt) is the same statement as A = P + Prt, just factored. Spreadsheets and financial calculators almost always use this factored form, and it generalizes cleanly to compound interest in Section 3.3.
3.2.2  When the time is shorter than a year

The rate in most simple-interest problems is quoted in percent per year. When the term is shorter than a year, the time t must be converted to a fraction of a year before being multiplied in. Six months is t = 0.5; 90 days is t = 90/365; 26 weeks is t = 26/52 = 0.5. Once the term is in the right unit, the formula does the rest.

Common mistakeTreating “6 months” as t = 6. The formula then computes six full years of interest and overshoots the answer by a factor of twelve. Always convert the term to years (or whatever unit the rate uses) before substituting.

Example 3.2. A 26-week T-bill at 4.5% on $10,000.

An investor buys a 26-week Treasury bill that promises a future value of $10,000. The Treasury Department prices T-bills using a simple-interest rate of 4.5% per year. We want the dollar amount of interest the investor will earn over the six-month term, and the purchase price the investor pays today.

Solution.

Step 1. Name the pieces. The future value is A = $10,000. The rate is r = 0.045 per year. The time is t = 26/52 = 0.5 years. The unknown is the principal P (the purchase price), and from that we can find the interest I = A − P.

Step 2. Solve A = P(1 + rt) for P.

P = A / (1 + r t) = 10,000 / (1 + 0.045 × 0.5)

Step 3. Compute the denominator and divide.

1 + 0.045 × 0.5 = 1.0225
P = 10,000 / 1.0225 ≈ 9,779.95

Step 4. Find the interest.

I = A − P = 10,000 − 9,779.95 = 220.05

The investor pays roughly $9,780 today and receives $10,000 at maturity, an interest payment of about $220 for six months of lending the money to the Treasury. Sanity check: 4.5% of $10,000 for a full year would be $450, and half a year produces roughly half of that, around $225, which is very close to the $220 we computed.
Simple interest is a straight line. Compound interest is a curve. Most of life lives on the curve.

In the wildUS Treasury bills, short-term commercial paper, and many auto-title loans quote interest in simple-interest form. So do informal “handshake” loans between family or friends. Anything longer than a year almost always switches to compound interest, because the interest-earning-interest effect of Section 3.3 becomes too valuable for lenders to ignore.

1. How much simple interest does $4,000 earn at 6% per year over 3 years?

    Show answer

    Answer. $720. I = Prt = 4,000 × 0.06 × 3 = 720.

  2. A $5,000 loan at 8% simple interest is repaid in 18 months. What is the total amount repaid?

    Show answer

    Answer. $5,600. Converting 18 months to years gives t = 1.5. The interest is I = 5,000 × 0.08 × 1.5 = 600, and A = 5,000 + 600 = 5,600.

  3. An investor pays $9,775 for a 26-week T-bill that matures at $10,000. What annual simple-interest rate does this represent? Round to two decimal places.

    Show answer

    Answer. About 4.60%. The interest is I = 10,000 − 9,775 = 225, and the time is t = 26/52 = 0.5 years. Solving r = I/(Pt) = 225/(9,775 × 0.5) ≈ 0.0460, or 4.60%.

  4. A simple-interest investment of $2,000 grows to $2,300 after 5 years. What annual rate does it pay?

    1. 2%
    2. 3%
    3. 5%
    4. 15%
    Show answer

    Answer. B (3%). The interest is I = 2,300 − 2,000 = 300, and r = 300/(2,000 × 5) = 0.03.

  5. What graph shape does a simple-interest account produce on a balance-versus-time plot?

    1. A horizontal line
    2. A straight, rising line
    3. A curve that bends upward
    4. A staircase
    Show answer

    Answer. B. The same dollar amount of interest is added each period, so the top of the balance climbs in a straight (positively sloped) line.

  6. If 6 months is the term and the rate is quoted per year, the correct value of t to put into I = Prt is…

    1. 6
    2. 0.5
    3. 1/6
    4. 0.06
    Show answer

    Answer. B (0.5). Six months is half a year, and the time must match the rate's units.

content.title: Simple interest, plug-and-go
content.problem: You deposit **$5,000** in a CD that pays **4% simple interest** for **3 years**. How much interest does it earn?
content.solution:
**Apply the simple interest formula I = Prt.**

I = (5,000)(0.04)(3) = **$600**.

The full payout at maturity is principal plus interest: $5,000 + $600 = **$5,600**.

Simple interest assumes you don't reinvest the interest as it accrues — it just sits there. That's why simple interest is rare in modern savings accounts (compound interest is the norm), but still common for short-term notes and T-bills.

content.title: Time units must match the rate
content.problem: A student takes out a **$1,200** loan at **6% simple interest** for **18 months**. How much interest accrues?
content.solution:
**Convert time to the unit the rate uses.** The rate is *per year*, so 18 months must be expressed as **1.5 years**.

I = (1,200)(0.06)(1.5) = **$108**.

If you accidentally plug in t = 18 (as months), you get I = 1,296 — over ten times too large. The unit-mismatch is one of the most common simple-interest errors.

*Rule of thumb.* If the rate is annual (the default), time must be in years. Convert months by dividing by 12, days by 365.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Simple interest grows linearly: I = Prt. Principal stays constant, time is always in the same unit as the rate (years, almost always). The full balance at maturity is **P(1 + rt)**. Best for short-term, fixed-term, no-compounding scenarios.

content.text: Simple interest keeps the formula painless, but it leaves money on the table. Most real-world accounts pay interest on the interest: every period, the accumulated interest joins the principal and earns more interest of its own. Section 3.3 introduces this compounding effect, runs it at four different frequencies, and shows the surprising place those frequencies head as they get faster and faster.
